items.prompt_md: Why does `combination_sum` sort `candidates` before starting the recursion, when subsets and permutations don't need a sorted input?
options: Sorting lets the loop prove, the moment `candidates[i] > remaining`, that every candidate after `i` is also too big — turning a wasted deeper search into an immediate `break`
Sorting removes duplicate values from the input automatically
Sorting is required for Python's recursion to terminate
Sorting changes the target sum to make the problem easier
items.answer: Sorting lets the loop prove, the moment `candidates[i] > remaining`, that every candidate after `i` is also too big — turning a wasted deeper search into an immediate `break`
items.explanation_md: Because the array is sorted ascending, once one candidate exceeds what's left to reach the target, everything to its right is guaranteed to be at least as large, so the whole remaining loop can be skipped in one step.

items.prompt_md: What job does the `start` parameter do in `backtrack(start, remaining)`?
options: It stops the search from looking backward at indices already passed, so a combination like `[2, 3]` is only ever built once and never also as `[3, 2]`
It tracks how many candidates have been used so far so the recursion can stop after a fixed count
It marks which candidates have already been used exactly once
It stores the running sum instead of `remaining`
items.answer: It stops the search from looking backward at indices already passed, so a combination like `[2, 3]` is only ever built once and never also as `[3, 2]`
items.explanation_md: By only ever recursing forward from the current position (or staying at it for reuse), the search treats `[2, 3]` and `[3, 2]` as the same combination instead of generating both.

items.prompt_md: Inside the loop, the recursive call is `backtrack(i, remaining - candidates[i])` — note it passes `i`, not `i + 1`. What does passing `i` allow?
options: It allows the same candidate to be chosen again in the next recursive step, which is how a combination like `2 + 2 + 3` becomes reachable
It skips candidate `i` entirely on the next call
It resets the search back to the very first candidate
It removes candidate `i` from the array permanently
items.answer: It allows the same candidate to be chosen again in the next recursive step, which is how a combination like `2 + 2 + 3` becomes reachable
items.explanation_md: Recursing at `i` (rather than `i + 1`) keeps candidate `i` eligible for the very next pick too, which is exactly what unlimited reuse of a value requires.

items.prompt_md: If the problem changed so each candidate could be used **at most once** per combination, what single change would the recursive call need?
options: Change `backtrack(i, remaining - candidates[i])` to `backtrack(i + 1, remaining - candidates[i])`, so the same index can never be chosen again in a deeper call
Remove the `start` parameter entirely
Sort the candidates in descending order instead of ascending
Replace the `for` loop with a `while` loop
items.answer: Change `backtrack(i, remaining - candidates[i])` to `backtrack(i + 1, remaining - candidates[i])`, so the same index can never be chosen again in a deeper call
items.explanation_md: Advancing past `i` (instead of staying at it) is the one-line change that turns 'reuse allowed' into 'use each element at most once', since it removes the option to pick the same index again.

items.prompt_md: Why does `if candidates[i] > remaining: break` work correctly, when a naive version might use `continue` instead?
options: Because the array is sorted, once one candidate is too big, every candidate after it in the loop is guaranteed to be too big as well, so there is nothing left in the loop worth checking — `break` exits immediately instead of wasting time checking each one with `continue`
Because `break` and `continue` behave identically in Python loops
Because `continue` would cause an infinite loop in this function
Because `break` also pops the current element off `path`
items.answer: Because the array is sorted, once one candidate is too big, every candidate after it in the loop is guaranteed to be too big as well, so there is nothing left in the loop worth checking — `break` exits immediately instead of wasting time checking each one with `continue`
items.explanation_md: `continue` would still visit every remaining (also too-large) candidate one at a time; `break` uses the sorted order to skip all of them at once, which is the whole point of sorting first.

items.prompt_md: What condition marks the base case where a valid combination gets recorded?
options: `remaining == 0`, meaning the candidates chosen so far sum exactly to the original target
`start == len(candidates)`
`len(path) == len(candidates)`
`remaining < 0`
items.answer: `remaining == 0`, meaning the candidates chosen so far sum exactly to the original target
items.explanation_md: The recursion tracks how much of the target is still unmet in `remaining`; hitting exactly zero means the current `path` is a valid combination worth copying into `result`.

items.prompt_md: A teammate's version appends `candidates[i]` to `path` and recurses, but forgets `path.pop()` afterward before the loop moves to the next `i`. What goes wrong?
options: Every later iteration of the loop keeps building on top of the leftover element instead of starting from the state before that choice, so combinations recorded afterward contain extra, incorrect elements
The function crashes with an `IndexError` immediately
The output is unaffected since `path` is only used for recursion depth, not content
The recursion never reaches the base case again
items.answer: Every later iteration of the loop keeps building on top of the leftover element instead of starting from the state before that choice, so combinations recorded afterward contain extra, incorrect elements
items.explanation_md: The un-choose step (`path.pop()`) is what restores `path` to its pre-choice state before trying the next candidate in the loop — skip it, and every subsequent branch inherits state it never actually chose.

items.prompt_md: Which statement best describes the worst-case time complexity of `combination_sum`?
options: It is exponential in the worst case — both the branching factor (number of candidates) and the depth (roughly target / smallest candidate) can grow, so there's no fixed formula like `2^n` or `n!`, only an exponential bound
It is always exactly `O(n log n)` because of the initial sort
It is `O(n)` because of the `break` pruning
It is `O(target)` regardless of how many candidates there are
items.answer: It is exponential in the worst case — both the branching factor (number of candidates) and the depth (roughly target / smallest candidate) can grow, so there's no fixed formula like `2^n` or `n!`, only an exponential bound
items.explanation_md: Unlike subsets (fixed 2 choices per level) or permutations (fixed n! total), combination sum's branching factor and depth both depend on the candidate values and target, so the honest claim is 'exponential', not a specific closed-form count.

items.prompt_md: Does the `break` pruning (stopping the loop once `candidates[i] > remaining`) change the worst-case asymptotic time complexity of the algorithm?
options: No — pruning reduces branches explored in practice and can be a large constant-factor speedup, but the worst-case bound is still exponential because a case with many small candidates can still force an exponential number of valid combinations
Yes — pruning always brings the complexity down to polynomial time
Yes — pruning changes the complexity to `O(log n)`
No — pruning has no effect on runtime at all, even in practice
items.answer: No — pruning reduces branches explored in practice and can be a large constant-factor speedup, but the worst-case bound is still exponential because a case with many small candidates can still force an exponential number of valid combinations
items.explanation_md: Pruning is a practical speedup, not an asymptotic one: an adversarial input (e.g., many copies of the value 1 and a large target) still forces an exponential number of valid combinations to be explored and recorded.

items.prompt_md: Why does carrying a running `remaining` total (rather than summing `path` from scratch at the base case) matter for efficiency?
options: It lets the search detect a dead branch — one that can never reach the target — before recursing further into it, instead of only discovering the failure after building a full, doomed path
It reduces the number of base cases from 2 to 1
It changes the time complexity from exponential to polynomial
It is only a style preference with no effect on performance
items.answer: It lets the search detect a dead branch — one that can never reach the target — before recursing further into it, instead of only discovering the failure after building a full, doomed path
items.explanation_md: A running total combined with sorted candidates is exactly what makes the early `break` possible — without it, the search would have no cheap way to tell a promising branch from a doomed one before going all the way down.

items.prompt_md: How does the *choice* made at each level of `combination_sum`'s recursion differ from the choice made at each level of the subsets recursion?
options: Subsets makes one binary include/exclude decision per fixed index; combination sum instead loops over multiple candidates at each level, choosing *which* one to add next (and possibly re-choosing the same one)
They are identical templates with no meaningful difference
Combination sum never uses a `path` list, only a running sum
Subsets allows reuse of elements while combination sum does not
items.answer: Subsets makes one binary include/exclude decision per fixed index; combination sum instead loops over multiple candidates at each level, choosing *which* one to add next (and possibly re-choosing the same one)
items.explanation_md: Subsets branches into exactly 2 children per level (in or out of one fixed element); combination sum branches into up to `len(candidates) - start` children per level, since the loop is choosing among several forward candidates rather than deciding on one fixed element.

items.prompt_md: Given a list of positive integers (values may be reused) and a target sum, you need every combination of numbers that adds up exactly to that target — not just whether one exists. What's the best approach?
options: Backtracking: sort the candidates, then choose an index (allowing reuse), recurse with the reduced target, and prune once the running sum can't fit
Two pointers converging from both ends of the sorted array
A single hash map lookup for the complement of the target
Binary search for the target sum directly
items.answer: Backtracking: sort the candidates, then choose an index (allowing reuse), recurse with the reduced target, and prune once the running sum can't fit
items.explanation_md: "Every combination that sums to a target, with reuse allowed" is the fingerprint for backtracking with a running-sum prune — two pointers and a single hash lookup only find one pair, not every combination of any length.

items.prompt_md: A vending-machine app must list every way to make exact change for an amount using an unlimited supply of coin denominations, so a customer can see all valid combinations of coins. What's the best approach?
options: Backtracking: sort the denominations, recurse choosing (with reuse) which coin to add next, and prune branches whose running total exceeds the amount
Greedy: always use the largest coin denomination that fits
A sliding window over the list of denominations
Reverse a linked list of denominations
items.answer: Backtracking: sort the denominations, recurse choosing (with reuse) which coin to add next, and prune branches whose running total exceeds the amount
items.explanation_md: Wanting every valid combination (not just the minimum number of coins) is what rules out greedy — this needs the full choose/recurse/un-choose enumeration with pruning on the running total.

items.prompt_md: You need to output every way to split a string into a sequence of palindromic substrings, considering every possible cut point. What's the best approach?
options: Backtracking: at each starting position, try every valid palindromic prefix, recurse on the rest of the string, and un-choose to try the next prefix length
A hash map counting character frequencies
Two pointers checking if the whole string is a palindrome
Binary search on the string length
items.answer: Backtracking: at each starting position, try every valid palindromic prefix, recurse on the rest of the string, and un-choose to try the next prefix length
items.explanation_md: Enumerating every way to partition a string (not just checking if one partition exists) is a choose/recurse/un-choose problem over cut points, structurally the same shape as combination sum's choose-index-and-recurse loop.

items.prompt_md: From a fixed deck of distinct cards, you need every group of exactly `k` cards (order within a group doesn't matter, no repeats), to enumerate all possible k-card hands. What's the best approach?
options: Backtracking: choose a starting index, recurse forward (never revisiting earlier indices) until the group has `k` cards, then un-choose to try the next option
Two pointers scanning from both ends of the deck
A stack that pushes and pops cards to match pairs
A hash set to remove duplicate cards
items.answer: Backtracking: choose a starting index, recurse forward (never revisiting earlier indices) until the group has `k` cards, then un-choose to try the next option
items.explanation_md: "Every group of size k, order doesn't matter, no repeats" is the combinations pattern — a forward-only start index prevents the same group from being built twice in different orders.

items.prompt_md: Given the head of a singly linked list, you need to detect whether it contains a cycle (a node whose `next` eventually loops back to an earlier node). What's the best approach?
options: Two pointers moving through the list at different speeds (slow and fast) — if they ever meet, there's a cycle
Backtracking: enumerate every possible sub-path through the list and check each for a repeat
A hash map from node index to sum of values
Sort the node values and use two pointers on the sorted result
items.answer: Two pointers moving through the list at different speeds (slow and fast) — if they ever meet, there's a cycle
items.explanation_md: This is a single structural yes/no question about a linked list, answerable with a fast/slow pointer walk in one pass — there's no combination of values to enumerate, so backtracking would be solving the wrong problem.

items.prompt_md: Given the root of a binary search tree, you need to check whether it satisfies the BST ordering property (every node's value lies within a valid range dictated by its ancestors). What's the best approach?
options: Recurse top-down through the tree, tightening the valid (low, high) bound passed to each child
Backtracking: try every possible reordering of the node values and check which one is valid
Two pointers converging on the tree's leftmost and rightmost nodes
A stack-based combination search over subtrees
items.answer: Recurse top-down through the tree, tightening the valid (low, high) bound passed to each child
items.explanation_md: Validating a single existing tree's structure is a tree-traversal problem with a bound carried down the recursion — it asks for a yes/no property check, not an enumeration of many possible combinations.

items.prompt_md: Given two arrays, you need to find the smallest set of numbers common to both, using a single pass with a lookup structure rather than comparing every pair. What's the best approach?
options: Build a hash set from one array, then scan the other checking membership
Backtracking: enumerate every combination of elements from both arrays and check for equality
Two pointers on unsorted arrays
A stack to track which elements have been visited
items.answer: Build a hash set from one array, then scan the other checking membership
items.explanation_md: A single membership/intersection question over arrays is solved with a hash set in one linear pass — enumerating combinations of pairs would be needless exponential work for a question with no requirement to list every combination.

items.prompt_md: You need to list every way to select a subset of `k` distinct problems from a bank of `n` practice problems for a study plan generator, where the order the problems are studied in doesn't matter. What's the best approach?
options: Backtracking: choose problems by index moving only forward, recursing until the selection has `k` problems, then un-choose to explore other selections
Sort the problems by difficulty and use two pointers
A greedy pass that always picks the hardest remaining problem
A hash map from problem id to difficulty score
items.answer: Backtracking: choose problems by index moving only forward, recursing until the selection has `k` problems, then un-choose to explore other selections
items.explanation_md: Enumerating every fixed-size, order-doesn't-matter selection from a set is exactly the forward-only-index combinations template, not a single-answer greedy or sorting problem.

items.prompt_md: A recipe app needs every combination of spice amounts (each spice can be used 0, 1, or more times up to a max) that hits a target total 'heat' score, so it can suggest all valid recipes. What's the best approach?
options: Backtracking: choose a spice (with possible reuse), recurse with a reduced target heat, and prune once the remaining heat can't be reached
Two pointers on a sorted list of heat scores
A single hash map lookup for the target heat
Binary search over spice combinations
items.answer: Backtracking: choose a spice (with possible reuse), recurse with a reduced target heat, and prune once the remaining heat can't be reached
items.explanation_md: Reuse-allowed combinations that must sum exactly to a target, where every valid combination is wanted, is the combination-sum shape: sort, choose with a forward-or-same index, and prune the running total.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array with possible duplicate values, you need to find any single pair of numbers whose sum equals a target, as efficiently as possible. What's the best approach?
options: Two pointers: one at each end, moving inward based on whether the current sum is too high or too low
Backtracking: recurse through every combination of two elements and un-choose the ones that don't sum to the target
A stack that pushes numbers until a pair is found
Recurse through every permutation of the array
items.answer: Two pointers: one at each end, moving inward based on whether the current sum is too high or too low
items.explanation_md: Finding a single qualifying pair in a sorted array is the two-pointers signature — an exhaustive backtracking search over combinations would be correct but needlessly exponential for a problem solvable in one linear pass.
